steps.description: Gentle non-comedogenic cleanser (cerave-foaming-facial-cleanser or cerave-hydrating-facial-cleanser). Twice daily.
Adapalene 0.1% gel (differin-adapalene-gel-01) nightly or Benzoyl Peroxide 2.5% (cerave-acne-foaming-cream-cleanser). Start slowly.
Add niacinamide 4–10% (the-ordinary-niacinamide-10-zinc-1 or cerave-pm-facial-moisturizing-lotion) for sebum regulation and anti-inflammatory support.
Topical retinoid (tretinoin 0.025–0.05%) or topical azelaic acid 15–20% if PIH is a concern.
Dermatologist evaluation for possible topical antibiotic + BPO combo, hormonal workup, or oral antibiotics.

special_populations.pregnancy: Avoid retinoids and salicylic acid. Use azelaic acid 20% or BPO as safe alternatives.
special_populations.pediatric: Adapalene 0.1% approved for ages ≥12. Avoid oral antibiotics in young children.
special_populations.skin_of_color: Prioritize gentle retinoids and azelaic acid to minimize PIH risk. Introduce actives gradually.
special_populations.sensitive: Start with lowest concentrations. Patch test BPO and retinoids. Consider azelaic acid first.

steps.description: Gentle cleanser + adapalene 0.1% + BPO 2.5% combination approach (differin-adapalene-gel-01 and a BPO wash).
Topical retinoid + topical antibiotic (clindamycin) with BPO. Or topical dapsone 5% gel for adult females.
Oral antibiotics (doxycycline 50–100 mg daily or sarecycline) for 3–4 months max, always with topical retinoid.
Hormonal therapy for females (spironolactone 50–200 mg/day or combined oral contraceptives).
Dermatologist referral for isotretinoin consideration if nodulocystic, scarring, or treatment-resistant.

special_populations.pregnancy: Avoid all retinoids, tetracyclines, spironolactone. Use BPO and azelaic acid only.
special_populations.pediatric: Avoid oral tetracyclines in children <8. Prefer topical therapy.
special_populations.skin_of_color: Monitor closely for PIH. Include azelaic acid. Avoid aggressive peels.
special_populations.sensitive: Buffer retinoids with moisturizer. Use lower-strength BPO.

steps.description: Immediate dermatologist referral. Oral isotretinoin 0.5–1 mg/kg/day is first-line for severe nodulocystic acne.
Intralesional corticosteroid injections (triamcinolone 2.5–5 mg/mL) for large nodules/cysts.
Photodynamic therapy (PDT) or blue/red light therapy as adjunct.

special_populations.pregnancy: Isotretinoin is absolutely contraindicated (Category X). Manage with BPO + azelaic acid; refer urgently.
special_populations.pediatric: Isotretinoin can be used in adolescents with close monitoring. iPLEDGE enrollment required.
special_populations.skin_of_color: High PIH and keloid risk. Emphasize gentle care and PIH prevention alongside acne treatment.
special_populations.sensitive: Isotretinoin often improves sensitivity by reducing oil. Supportive moisturizers (cerave-moisturizing-cream, eltamd-pm-therapy) essential.

steps.description: Foundation: fragrance-free cleanser + liberal moisturizer within 3 minutes of bathing. Ceramide-rich creams preferred (cerave-moisturizing-cream or skinceuticals-triple-lipid-restore-242).
Colloidal oatmeal baths or creams for soothing (aveno-eczema-therapy). Low-potency hydrocortisone 1% for flares.
Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.03–0.1% or pimecrolimus 1%) for face/folds and maintenance.
Crisaborole 2% ointment (Eucrisa) or ruxolitinib 1.5% cream for mild-moderate AD.

special_populations.pregnancy: Emollients and low-potency TCS are safe. Avoid high-potency steroids and systemic agents.
special_populations.pediatric: Tacrolimus 0.03% approved for ages ≥2. Emollients safe from birth. Dupilumab approved ≥6 months.
special_populations.skin_of_color: Follicular pattern common. PIH risk with steroids; prefer TCI on face. Lichenified plaques may appear gray.
special_populations.sensitive: All AD patients are sensitive. Avoid fragrance, essential oils, harsh surfactants.

steps.description: Intensive emollient regimen + medium-potency steroid (triamcinolone 0.1%) for flares on trunk/extremities.
Topical calcineurin inhibitors for face/folds. Wet wrap therapy for severe flares.
Phototherapy (NB-UVB) 2–3x weekly for widespread disease.
Dupilumab (Dupixent) biweekly injection — first-line biologic for moderate-severe AD.

special_populations.pregnancy: Dupilumab generally considered safe (limited data). Avoid systemic immunosuppressants.
special_populations.pediatric: Dupilumab approved for children ≥6 months. Tacrolimus 0.03% for ≥2 years.
special_populations.skin_of_color: Higher risk of PIH. Prioritize TCI over TCS on face. Monitor for follicular pattern.
special_populations.sensitive: All patients sensitive by definition. Use steroid-sparing strategies where possible.

steps.description: Urgent dermatologist referral. Dupilumab or tralokinumab biologic therapy.
Oral JAK inhibitors (upadacitinib or abrocitinib) for severe refractory AD.
Phototherapy (NB-UVB or UVA1). Wet wrap therapy for acute flares.

special_populations.pregnancy: Cyclosporine may be used if necessary with close monitoring. Most biologics/JAK inhibitors limited data.
special_populations.pediatric: Dupilumab approved ≥6 months. JAK inhibitors approved for adolescents/adults.
special_populations.skin_of_color: Higher PIH and keloid risk. Gentle treatment essential. Phototherapy response may differ.
special_populations.sensitive: Systemic therapies may reduce need for topicals, decreasing irritation risk.

steps.description: Gentle, non-irritating cleanser (cerave-hydrating-facial-cleanser or tatcha-camellia-cleansing-oil). Avoid sulfates, hot water, scrubs.
Mineral sunscreen daily (eltamd-uv-clear-spf-46 or eltamd-uv-elements-spf-44). Zinc oxide is anti-inflammatory and well-tolerated.
steps.duration: Daily, reapply every 2 hours in sun

steps.description: Azelaic acid 10% OTC or niacinamide 4–5% for redness and bumps (the-ordinary-niacinamide-10-zinc-1 or cerave-pm-facial-moisturizing-lotion).
Metronidazole 0.75–1% cream/gel or azelaic acid 15% gel for papulopustular rosacea.

special_populations.pregnancy: Azelaic acid and metronidazole are generally considered safe. Avoid ivermectin and brimonidine data limited.
special_populations.pediatric: Rosacea uncommon in children. Pediatric dermatology referral if suspected.
special_populations.skin_of_color: May present with darker, hyperpigmented papules rather than classic erythema. Post-inflammatory pigmentation common.
special_populations.sensitive: All rosacea skin is sensitive. Avoid acids, retinoids, benzoyl peroxide, scrubs, hot water.

steps.description: Topical ivermectin 1% cream (Soolantra) for papules/pustules. Or topical metronidazole + azelaic acid.
Low-dose oral doxycycline 40 mg (Oracea) — anti-inflammatory sub-antimicrobial dose for papulopustular rosacea.
Brimonidine 0.33% gel (Mirvaso) or oxymetazoline 1% cream (Rhofade) for persistent facial erythema.

special_populations.pregnancy: Avoid oral tetracyclines. Topical metronidazole and azelaic acid preferred.
special_populations.pediatric: Rare in children; refer to pediatric dermatologist.
special_populations.skin_of_color: Hyperpigmentation may be primary concern. Treat inflammation to prevent PIH.
special_populations.sensitive: Introduce brimonidine cautiously — rebound redness can occur in some patients.

steps.description: Dermatologist referral for severe papulopustular or ocular rosacea. Oral isotretinoin may be considered (low dose).
Vascular laser (pulsed dye laser 585–595 nm or KTP laser) for persistent erythema and telangiectasias.

special_populations.pregnancy: Avoid oral isotretinoin and tetracyclines. Manage with topicals only.
special_populations.pediatric: Rare; urgent pediatric dermatology referral.
special_populations.skin_of_color: Laser settings must be conservative to avoid PIH. Test spots mandatory.
special_populations.sensitive: Low-dose isotretinoin often tolerated. Intensive moisturization essential.

steps.description: Strict photoprotection: broad-spectrum SPF 30+ daily, reapply every 2 hours. Mineral sunscreen with iron oxides preferred for Fitzpatrick IV–VI (eltamd-uv-clear-spf-46 or eltamd-uv-elements-spf-44).
Azelaic acid 10% or niacinamide 4–5% (the-ordinary-niacinamide-10-zinc-1, the-ordinary-alpha-arbutin-2-ha) for gentle lightening.
Azelaic acid 20% cream or hydroquinone 2–4% for 8–12 weeks with strict photoprotection.

special_populations.pregnancy: Avoid hydroquinone and retinoids. Use azelaic acid, vitamin C, niacinamide, physical sunscreen only.
special_populations.pediatric: Rare in children; investigate hormonal causes if present.
special_populations.skin_of_color: Iron oxide-containing tinted sunscreens critical. Visible light protection essential. Conservative procedures only.
special_populations.sensitive: Azelaic acid and niacinamide are well-tolerated. Avoid high-strength AHAs.

steps.description: Triple combination cream (hydroquinone 4% + tretinoin 0.05% + mild steroid) for 8 weeks.
Add oral tranexamic acid 250 mg BID (off-label) for refractory melasma, after thrombosis screening.
Cysteamine 5% cream (Cyspera) — non-hydroquinone option for maintenance.

special_populations.pregnancy: Avoid triple combo (HQ + retinoid). Use azelaic acid 20% as primary.
special_populations.pediatric: Rare; refer to dermatologist.
special_populations.skin_of_color: High risk of worsening with procedures. Lasers/peels only by expert with test spots.
special_populations.sensitive: Triple combo can irritate. Buffer with moisturizer. Start every other night.

steps.description: Dermatologist referral. Oral tranexamic acid + topical triple combination or azelaic acid 20%.
Very conservative chemical peels (low-strength glycolic or salicylic acid series) ONLY by experienced provider. Test spots mandatory.
Low-fluence Q-switched Nd:YAG 1064 nm or fractional laser ONLY as last resort by melasma specialist. High PIH risk.

special_populations.pregnancy: No procedures during pregnancy. Manage with azelaic acid and sunscreen only.
special_populations.pediatric: Rare; refer to specialist.
special_populations.skin_of_color: Procedures carry highest risk. Visible light protection (iron oxides) is non-negotiable. Maintenance for life.
special_populations.sensitive: Avoid all procedures if sensitive. Focus on topicals + sun protection.

steps.description: Daily SPF 30+ mineral sunscreen (eltamd-uv-clear-spf-46). Prevention is paramount.
Vitamin C serum AM (skinceuticals-ce-ferulic or the-ordinary-ascorbic-acid-8-alpha-arbutin-2) + niacinamide PM (the-ordinary-niacinamide-10-zinc-1).
Hydroquinone 2–4% for 8–12 weeks OR azelaic acid 20% for longer-term maintenance.

special_populations.pregnancy: Avoid HQ and retinoids. Use vitamin C, azelaic acid, niacinamide, physical sunscreen.
special_populations.pediatric: Focus on treating underlying condition (acne, eczema) to prevent new PIH.
special_populations.skin_of_color: PIH is the rule. Prevention > treatment. Gentle approach. Avoid aggressive procedures.
special_populations.sensitive: Vitamin C and niacinamide are generally well-tolerated. Patch test HQ.

steps.description: HQ 4% + tretinoin 0.025–0.05% combination. Use at night with strict daytime SPF.
Add oral tranexamic acid 250 mg BID (after thrombosis screening) for refractory PIH.
Conservative chemical peel series (salicylic acid 20–30% or mandelic acid 20–40%). Multiple sessions.

special_populations.pregnancy: Avoid HQ, retinoids, oral TXA. Manage with azelaic acid and vitamin C only.
special_populations.pediatric: Address underlying inflammatory condition first.
special_populations.skin_of_color: Mandelic acid preferred over glycolic due to slower penetration. Test spots mandatory.
special_populations.sensitive: Peels may irritate. Start with lowest strength. Consider microdermabrasion instead.

steps.description: Emollient moisturizers (cerave-moisturizing-cream, skinceuticals-triple-lipid-restore-242) to reduce scaling and itch.
Coal tar preparations or salicylic acid shampoos for scalp. OTC topical steroids (hydrocortisone 1%) for limited plaques.
Topical corticosteroids (betamethasone dipropionate) + vitamin D analogs (calcipotriene) for body plaques.

special_populations.pregnancy: Topical steroids and calcipotriene are generally safe. Avoid systemic agents.
special_populations.pediatric: Topicals first-line. Avoid potent steroids. Limited calcipotriene use.
special_populations.skin_of_color: May appear violaceous or hyperpigmented rather than classic erythema. PIH risk with irritation.
special_populations.sensitive: Start with lower-potency steroids. Vitamin D analogs may irritate sensitive skin.

steps.description: Phototherapy (NB-UVB) 2–3x weekly. First-line for moderate widespread psoriasis.
Systemic non-biologics: methotrexate 7.5–25 mg weekly OR apremilast (Otezla) OR cyclosporine short-term.
Biologic agents: TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept) or IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab).

special_populations.pregnancy: NB-UVB is safe. Avoid methotrexate, acitretin, biologics in 3rd trimester.
special_populations.pediatric: Etanercept, adalimumab, ustekinumab approved for pediatric psoriasis. Phototherapy safe.
special_populations.skin_of_color: May present with gray scale and violaceous hue. Higher nail involvement. Scalp psoriasis common.
special_populations.sensitive: Phototherapy is well-tolerated. Biologics eliminate need for irritating topicals.

steps.description: Urgent dermatology referral. Biologic agents: IL-23 inhibitors (guselkumab, risankizumab) or IL-17 inhibitors.
Combination therapy: biologic + methotrexate (enhances efficacy, reduces immunogenicity).
Phototherapy (NB-UVB or PUVA) as adjunct. Targeted excimer laser for resistant plaques.

special_populations.pregnancy: Avoid systemic steroids (rebound risk). NB-UVB preferred. Cyclosporine if necessary.
special_populations.pediatric: Refer to pediatric dermatologist. Etanercept and ustekinumab approved.
special_populations.skin_of_color: Higher risk of PIH with phototherapy. Monitor closely. Scalp and nail involvement common.
special_populations.sensitive: Biologics minimize need for topical irritation. Systemic therapy often best option.

steps.description: Anti-dandruff shampoo 2–3x weekly (ketoconazole 1%, zinc pyrithione, selenium sulfide). Leave on 3–5 minutes before rinsing.
Gentle facial cleanser + light moisturizer. Avoid oils that feed Malassezia (coconut, olive).
Ketoconazole 2% cream or ciclopirox 0.77% gel for facial/seborrheic areas.

special_populations.pregnancy: Topical ketoconazole and zinc pyrithione are generally considered safe.
special_populations.pediatric: Cradle cap: mineral oil + gentle brushing. Avoid adult-strength antifungals on infants.
special_populations.skin_of_color: May present with hypopigmentation or hyperpigmentation rather than classic erythema.
special_populations.sensitive: Avoid harsh sulfates and fragrances. Gentle antifungal approach.

steps.description: Ketoconazole 2% shampoo/cream + low-potency corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1–2.5%) for inflammation.
Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.1% or pimecrolimus 1%) for steroid-sparing maintenance, especially on face.
Oral antifungal (itraconazole 200 mg daily for 7 days) for severe or refractory cases.

special_populations.pregnancy: Avoid oral antifungals. Topical ketoconazole + hydrocortisone only.
special_populations.pediatric: Refer to pediatric dermatologist for moderate-severe cases.
special_populations.skin_of_color: Post-inflammatory pigment changes common. Treat inflammation to prevent pigment issues.
special_populations.sensitive: Calcineurin inhibitors are excellent steroid-sparing option for sensitive facial skin.

steps.description: Urea 10–20% cream or lactic acid 12% lotion for keratolytic exfoliation. Apply daily.
Salicylic acid 2% body wash or gluconolactone (PHA) cleanser (cerave-renewing-sa-cleanser) for gentle follicular exfoliation.
Ceramide moisturizer after exfoliation to support barrier (cerave-moisturizing-cream).
Topical retinoid (tretinoin 0.025% or adapalene 0.1%) for persistent cases.

special_populations.pregnancy: Avoid retinoids. Use urea and lactic acid only.
special_populations.pediatric: Urea and lactic acid safe for children. Start with lower concentrations.
special_populations.skin_of_color: Higher risk of PIH with aggressive exfoliation. Use gentle, consistent approach.
special_populations.sensitive: PHAs (gluconolactone) are gentler than AHAs/BHAs. Patch test urea and lactic acid.

steps.description: Discontinue all topical steroids, heavy moisturizers, and fluoridated toothpaste. Use SLS-free, fragrance-free cleanser (cerave-hydrating-facial-cleanser).
Topical metronidazole 0.75% gel or azelaic acid 15–20% for papules.
Low-dose oral tetracycline (doxycycline 40–100 mg/day or minocycline 50–100 mg/day) for 6–12 weeks.

special_populations.pregnancy: Avoid oral tetracyclines. Use topical metronidazole or azelaic acid.
special_populations.pediatric: More common than thought in children. Avoid topical steroids. Gentle cleansers + topical metronidazole.
special_populations.skin_of_color: May present with hyperpigmented papules rather than classic erythema. PIH is common.
special_populations.sensitive: Minimal routine essential. No actives beyond prescribed treatment.

steps.description: Identify and remove offending agent. Gentle fragrance-free cleanser + barrier repair moisturizer (cerave-moisturizing-cream or skinceuticals-triple-lipid-restore-242).
Cool compresses. Colloidal oatmeal baths/soaks for soothing.
Low- to medium-potency topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1–2.5% or triamcinolone 0.1%) for 1–2 weeks.
Patch testing by dermatologist for allergic contact dermatitis. Refer if persistent or recurrent.

special_populations.pregnancy: Low-potency topical steroids are safe. Avoid systemic agents and extensive steroid use.
special_populations.pediatric: Hydrocortisone 1% preferred. Avoid medium/high-potency steroids on children.
special_populations.skin_of_color: Irritant and allergic contact dermatitis may cause significant PIH. Treat promptly to prevent pigment issues.
special_populations.sensitive: All patients with contact dermatitis have sensitive skin during flare. Minimal, fragrance-free routine essential.

steps.description: Medium-potency topical steroid (triamcinolone 0.1% or betamethasone valerate 0.1%) for 1–2 weeks.
Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.1% or pimecrolimus 1%) for face, eyelids, or maintenance.
Oral corticosteroid short course (prednisone taper) for widespread allergic contact dermatitis.

special_populations.pregnancy: Avoid oral steroids if possible. Topical hydrocortisone only.
special_populations.pediatric: Avoid oral steroids. Hydrocortisone 1% + wet wraps for moderate cases.
special_populations.skin_of_color: High PIH risk. Treat aggressively but gently. Patch testing essential.
special_populations.sensitive: TCI are preferred for long-term management to avoid steroid side effects.
